data.name: Borderlands Quest: Dagger Danger!
entries: {@i A short adventure for level 1 characters}

{@i Borderlands Quest: Dagger Danger} is a Dungeons & Dragons adventure that introduces four or five new players to the game and teaches you, the Dungeon Master, how to run it for them. This short adventure is meant to take two hours to play.
The adventure uses elements from the {@adventure D&D Starter Set: Heroes of the Borderlands|HotB}, allowing people who have that set to incorporate those elements, if desired.

entries.name: The Basics
entries: Dungeons & Dragons (D&D) is a collaborative storytelling and roleplaying game. Each player takes on the role of an adventuring character: a hero with skill and knowledge—and sometimes magic—that lets them overcome challenges and achieve greatness!

One of the participants in the game takes the role of the Dungeon Master (DM). The DM acts as the lead storyteller, the keeper of secrets, and the referee. This document provides everything you need to know to get started as a DM.

entries.name: The Rules
entries: Dungeons & Dragons is a special type of game known as a roleplaying game (RPG). In D&D, the rules help determine if the characters successfully face challenges or encounter setbacks in their adventures. The rules also help the players tell a fun, exciting, and memorable story. The rules are explained as they come up in this adventure.

entries.name: The Tools
entries: To play this game, you need this adventure and dice: a {@dice d20}, {@dice d12}, {@dice d10}, {@dice d8}, {@dice d6}, and {@dice d4}. If you don't have these dice, you can find digital dice rollers online. Each player should also have a way to take notes and keep track of changes to their character sheet as the game progresses.

entries.name: Preparation
entries: Before running the adventure, prepare as follows:

entries.name: Step 2
entries: Have your players create or claim level 1 characters on D&D Beyond.

entries.name: Step 3
entries: Review the monster stat blocks below.

entries.name: Dungeon Mastering This Adventure
entries: To play this adventure, you'll need either the {@link D&D Beyond Basic Rules|https://www.dndbeyond.com/sources/dnd/br-2024} or the {@book Player's Handbook|XPHB}, {@book Dungeon Master's Guide|XDMG}, and {@book Monster Manual|XMM}.

entries.name: Helpful Sidebars
entries: Throughout the adventure, you'll find sidebar boxes like the one below with helpful information and tips on adventure presentation and rules.

entries.name: Read-Aloud Text
entries: Text inside a box, like the text below, is meant to be read aloud to the players. If you're comfortable doing so, you can summarize or paraphrase the text. Just be sure to mention important information—especially when the text is describing the setting the characters are in. It's hard for them to interact with the world and story when they don't know what's there to interact with!

Text that appears in a box like this is meant to be read aloud or paraphrased for the players when their characters first arrive at a location or under a specific circumstance, as described in the text.

entries.name: Introducing Characters
entries: To run this adventure, first present the character sheets to the players and read the read-aloud text below, which covers the basics of a character sheet.

You should use the {@link premade characters|https://www.dndbeyond.com/characters/premade} available on D&D Beyond. If you're an experienced Dungeon Master, you might wish to make another group of level 1 characters for your players to claim on D&D Beyond.

These characters are the heroes you can play in this adventure. The words and numbers on the sheets describe who the characters are, what they're good at doing, and other important information. Choose the hero you'd like to play. As we play through this adventure, we'll talk more about what these words and numbers mean. Let's discuss some basics first.

entries.name: Name
entries: There's a space at the top of your character sheet where you can write your character's name.

entries.name: Species, Background, and Class
entries: Each character in D&D has a {@book Species|XPHB|4|Species Descriptions}, {@book Background|XPHB|4|Background Descriptions}, and {@book Class|XPHB|3} that help determine who a character is and what they do best. Other information on a character sheet is based on these three elements.

entries.name: Abilities and Ability Scores
entries: All creatures (including your characters) have six abilities: Strength, Dexterity, Constitution, Intelligence, Wisdom, and Charisma. Each of the ability scores governs a specific characteristic. Strength is physical might; Dexterity is agility, reflexes, and balance; Constitution is health and stamina; Intelligence is reasoning and memory; Wisdom is perceptiveness and mental fortitude, and Charisma is confidence, poise, and charm.

Each ability has a score from 1 to 20, although your characters will have scores ranging from 8 (weaker than human average) to 16 or more (strong capability). Each of those scores has an ability modifier next to it. Whenever you make a {@variantrule D20 Test|XPHB} (a {@dice d20} roll to determine success or failure) with a specific ability, you'll modify the roll by the number appearing there.

entries.name: Initiative
entries: Each character has an {@variantrule Initiative|XPHB} modifier. When we get to a point in the adventure where we have to take turns—particularly during combat—I'll tell you to "roll Initiative." When this happens, roll a {@dice d20} and add your character's Initiative modifier.

After we get everyone's Initiative, I'll arrange them in order, starting with the character with the highest Initiative roll. From there, you act in descending order. After everyone acts, that's the end of the round. The Initiative order remains the same from round to round for the whole combat. If there's a tie, I'll decide which character or creature goes before the other.

entries.name: The Characters
entries: The following text contains a more complete discussion of the character sheet. Read this text to the players for a thorough discussion of the characters' abilities:

entries.name: Character Actions
entries: A character's actions hold the potential for either success or failure. Can you hit a monster with your weapon? Can you balance on a narrow ledge as you ascend a mountain? Can you convince a dragon not to eat you? You find the answer to these questions during the game by making a {@variantrule D20 Test|XPHB}!

entries.name: Attacks
entries: Each character can make attacks with melee or ranged weapons, or with melee or ranged spells. "Melee" means an attack that is used when a character is right next to a foe. "Ranged" means an attack that is used at a distance. Ranged attacks include the distance you must be within to hit a foe.

The number after the attack's name on your character sheet is added to the {@dice d20} roll (a roll of a 20-sided die) you make when your character attacks. The higher the number, the more likely the attack is to succeed.

entries.name: Armor Class and Hit Points
entries: Each character has an {@variantrule Armor Class|XPHB} (also called AC) and {@variantrule Hit Points|XPHB} (also called HP). A character's Armor Class is based on their speed, armor, and other factors and describes how difficult it is to hit them with an attack roll. The higher the AC, the harder they are to hit. Hit Points determine how much damage a character can take. When your character takes damage, you subtract that damage from their Hit Points. If a character's Hit Points reach 0, they are unconscious. You'll learn how to regain Hit Points later in the game.

entries.name: Skills
entries: Each character can use the skills listed on their character sheet. The number after a skill shows what the player adds to a {@dice d20} roll. The higher the number, the better the character is at that skill. If a character wants to use a skill that isn't on their character sheet, the player rolls a 20-sided die, adding an ability score modifier if the DM tells them that ability applies.

entries.name: Equipment
entries: A character has equipment they can use to overcome challenges. They also might find more equipment during their adventures.

entries.name: Spells or Other Abilities
entries: Each character has spells, special features, or other abilities. You get to choose when your character uses their spells or special abilities. However, each character can use their spells and special abilities either a limited number of times or only under special circumstances. If there's a small box next to a spell or ability, that means you can only use it that many times. Check off a box when you use it to track how many uses you have left.

entries.name: Tips for Dungeon Masters
entries: Here are some general tips for new Dungeon Masters.

entries.name: Rule 0
entries: Rule 0 of D&D is simple: Have fun. It's fine if everyone agrees to change the rules as long as doing so means the game is more fun for everyone.

entries.name: Be Supportive
entries: The players and the DM are all on the same team. DMs aren't playing against the characters. The DM wins when the players have fun and the story is exciting and memorable.

{@b Use "Yes, and ..." or "No, but ..."} Allow the players to succeed as much as possible and participate in the story. If they want to try something unexpected, try to say "yes" and work their ideas into the story. If you have to say "no" to an idea, suggest options that might work.

entries.name: Use Your Imagination
entries: Adventures are an outline for your game. Change or add anything that makes the game more fun. What does a monster smell like? That's up to you, or you can ask the players what they think!

entries.name: NPCs Are Your Voice
entries: While the players determine what their characters do, the DM controls the other characters in the game. Nonplayer characters (also known as NPCs) are a great tool for you to help the characters understand the game and the story, and to work information into the story smoothly.

entries.name: Allow Alternatives
entries: D&D is a game of fantasy where heroes use wits, skill, and determination to overcome obstacles. Sometimes those obstacles are defeated with weapons and spells, but characters can succeed in other ways. Communicating with monsters, tricking them or frightening them away, or avoiding a fight while cleverly sneaking past a challenge can be just as much fun.

entries.name: Listen to Your Players
entries: Encourage your players to speak to you, publicly or privately, if something in the game upsets them. Then respond appropriately. For instance, you can move past upsetting topics by quickly narrating a resolution to a scene and then moving on to the next part of the story.

entries.name: More DM Advice
entries: Here are some more advanced tips for Dungeon Masters.

entries.name: Play Time
entries: Learning to pace an adventure to make sure that it runs in a set time frame takes practice, but here are some tips to help.

Give everyone an opportunity to make comments and discuss the game as it plays. Once everyone has had the chance to give input, ask questions, and discuss a game situation, prompt the players to make a decision and keep the game moving forward.
To prompt the players, summarize the situation as they understand it, and then say, "What do you do?"

entries.name: Presenting Encounters
entries: This adventure is divided into "encounters," which are like scenes from a movie or show. Each encounter has specific challenges. Once the players complete the challenges, move on to the next scene. The flow of the game is described below.

entries.name: Game Flow
entries: A D&D session begins with the DM and the other players having a conversation. The DM explains the situation the characters are in. The players can ask questions about the situation, and the DM answers those questions to offer clarity. Then the DM asks the most important question in the DM's toolbox:

{@b "What do you do?"} At this point, the players explain what they want their characters to do. (If what they want to do is impossible, tell them so and ask them to choose a different action. For example, a character can't walk through a wall unless they have some special ability or magic that allows them to do so.)
If the action is very easy to do, the character automatically succeeds. Opening an unlocked door or lifting a light object doesn't require heroic effort, so no {@dice d20} roll is necessary.

When the action is somewhere between easy and impossible, trying to do it could lead to success or failure. That's when the DM calls for a {@dice d20} roll. (See the D20 Tests section earlier in this document.) This roll, plus its modifiers, determines whether the character succeeds or fails. What does success or failure mean for an adventure? This is where you and the players get to tell a story together!

entries.name: Narrating Successes and Failures
entries: When a {@dice d20} roll succeeds or fails, the DM and the players can turn that result into a part of the ongoing story. What does it look like when a monster is defeated? What does the Gnome Barbarian say or do if the heavy boulder blocking the cave doesn't budge? These points in the game can spark great storytelling and acting moments.

Remember, though, that part of the DM's job is to keep the game's story moving forward. If describing every single attack roll in a long combat gets boring, just say how much damage the attack deals and get to the next exciting moment.

entries.name: Introducing the Adventure
entries: Once the players have familiarized themselves with their characters, you're ready to introduce the game.

Now that you're familiar with your characters and the basics of the rules, we're going to play a short adventure to introduce you to Dungeons & Dragons. As the Dungeon Master, I tell you what's going on in the world around you, and I use the rules and dice rolls to determine what happens in the story. But the most important aspect of the game is your input. You help tell the story of your characters' adventures.

In a moment, I'll describe the scene you're in, and then you'll tell me what you want your character to do. Your character can try to do anything, but each character is specially trained to do specific things well, so think about what your character might be more successful doing. Their skills, weapons, spells, equipment, and abilities are listed and described on your character sheet.

When your character does something that might succeed or might fail, I'll have you roll one of these 20-sided dice and add a number to it. This is called a {@variantrule D20 Test|XPHB}, and the number you add indicates how good your character is at what they're trying to do. The result tells me whether your character succeeds or fails. Then I'll describe the outcome, and the story moves forward. Now let's begin playing this adventure! It's called {@i Borderlands Quest: Dagger Danger}!

entries.name: More Rules Details
entries: Below are some rules details that come up in this adventure:

items.name: D20 Tests
items.entry: The 20-sided die ({@dice d20}) is the most-used die in D&D. It is rolled whenever a character or a creature tries to do something important. Things a character might try to do are listed under "Attacks" and "Skills" on the character sheets. If a character is about to be attacked or influenced by a physical or mental effect, they might need to make a "{@variantrule Saving Throw|XPHB}."

entries: Next to attacks, skills, and saving throws are numbers, such as "+5." This is the number a player adds to their die roll when their character uses that attack, skill, or saving throw. If the die roll is a 10, the +5 bonus is added, and the final total for the attack roll is 15.
How does the player know if their roll succeeds or fails? That's determined by the {@variantrule Armor Class|XPHB} or Difficulty Class, which are described below!

items.name: Armor Class
items.entry: The number a character tries to equal or exceed with an attack roll is called Armor Class (also called AC). The higher a creature's Armor Class, the harder that creature is to hit. In the previous example, the attack roll would hit a creature with an Armor Class of 15 or lower.

items.name: Damage
items.entry: When an attack roll hits, it deals damage. The damage roll appears after the attack on the character sheet. If a Greataxe does "{@dice 1d12 + 3} Slashing," the player would roll one 12-sided die ({@dice d12}) and add 3 to the die roll. The DM subtracts the total from the creature's Hit Points.

items.name: Hit Points
items.entry: Every creature in D&D has a number of {@variantrule Hit Points|XPHB}. This number reflects the damage a creature can take before it falls unconscious or is defeated. When a character or creature is damaged by an attack, trap, or hazard in the game, the amount of damage dealt is subtracted from the character or creature's Hit Points.

entries: If a creature reaches 0 Hit Points, they are defeated. The players can decide if this means a creature is dead or just {@condition Unconscious|XPHB}.

When a character reaches 0 Hit Points, they are unconscious. In this adventure, a character at 0 Hit Points remains unconscious until they regain Hit Points through healing or until the end of the encounter, at which point they regain 1 Hit Point automatically. The adventure details other ways to regain Hit Points.

items.name: Difficulty Class
items.entry: Like the Armor Class determining whether an attack roll succeeds or fails, the Difficulty Class (DC) determines if an ability check or saving throw succeeds or fails. The DC of an ability check or saving throw is the number that a player's roll must equal or exceed to succeed.

entries.name: Starting the Adventure
entries: Begin the adventure by reading the following to the players.

Yesterday you saw a quest posted on a quest board at the Keep on the Borderlands. The quest was posted by Quintanna Zorn, an adventurer with a strange request. The quest post asked someone to go to the Kobold Cave, one of the mysterious and dangerous Caves of Chaos, and retrieve a valuable heirloom that Zorn dropped when she was there seven days ago.

She and her group defeated a group of kobolds there and looted their belongings. They'd also found a baby dragon being kept by the kobolds, which they took into the wilderness to release safely. Zorn will be back in a few days, so in the meantime she posted a quest for someone to retrieve that dagger.

The dagger is a Zorn family heirloom, according to the quest posting. It's not magical or even very valuable, but it has sentimental value to Quintanna. It's a simple steel dagger with an opal set into the pommel. The post promised a reward of 100 Gold Pieces to the person or group who brings the dagger to Zorn at the Traveler's Inn when she returns from her trip.
Now you stand in front of the Kobold Cave, ready to retrieve the dagger and get the reward.
Let the players ask any questions they might have about the events leading up to their arrival. After you've answered their questions, continue to "Cave Entrance."

entries.name: The Adventure Hook
entries: An adventure hook is the challenge, opportunity, situation, or conflict that "hooks" the players into an adventure, motivating them to pursue it. It introduces the characters to the adventure: what's happening, where they are, what they know, what their goals are, and so on.

This adventure hook starts the characters at the point where they have already done some of the work in finding the location, and they are ready to jump into the main part of the adventure. When you start an adventure this way, it generally gets the players into the action faster, but it might also bring some questions.

Sometimes the adventure text answers these questions, and other times it doesn't—because there's no way the text can accommodate every question a player might ask. One of the challenges, and one of the joys, of being the Dungeon Master is getting to add your imagination to the story.

entries.name: Kobold Lair Features
entries: The cave has the following features:

items: {@b Ceilings}. The cave's ceilings are 10 feet high.
{@b Environment}. The cave has rough stone walls and a dirt floor. The floor is littered with gnawed animal bones and junk left by the kobolds.

{@b Lighting}. The cave is unlit, and its denizens rely on {@sense Darkvision|XPHB} or other means to see. Area descriptions assume the characters have a light source or other means of seeing in {@variantrule Darkness|XPHB}.

entries.name: Looking Back?
entries: Starting right at the entrance to the cave gets to the action more quickly, which many players enjoy. Some players, however, want to know what their characters know, including what they might have done or asked in the time between reading the quest post and arriving at the cave. Managing those flashback questions well comes with experience. Here are some potential questions and how to handle them:
{@i Could I have bought something before we left?}

If the item was something that you think would be a common piece of adventuring equipment worth 10 GP or less, allow the player to subtract the cost and add it to their inventory. A list of items suitable for purchase can be found in the {@link D&D Beyond Basic Rules|https://www.dndbeyond.com/sources/dnd/br-2024} under "{@book Equipment|XPHB|6}."
{@i Could we find out more about the Caves of Chaos}?
The caves are near the keep, and dangerous creatures are known to dwell there. No one the characters could have talked to knows about the Kobold Cave specifically.
{@i Do we know anything about Quintanna Zorn}?

The characters don't know Zorn personally, and the people they ask about her say she is a trustworthy, seasoned adventurer. You can make up the other details or change them to fit a story you want to tell.

entries.name: A1: Cave Entrance
entries: When the characters examine the cave's entrance, read or paraphrase the following:

A twenty-foot-long tunnel leads into the cave. Halfway into the tunnel is a ten-foot-wide, ten-foot-deep pit, making the path forward dangerous. Beyond the pit, the cave is dark and silent, and the floor glistens with dampness.
The characters can examine the entry of this cave to learn more about who passed through here and what might await them inside.

items: {@b Searching the Area}. No D20 Test is needed to learn that this entrance has not been traveled much recently. If a character takes the {@action Search|XPHB} action and succeeds on a {@dc 15} Wisdom ({@skill Perception|XPHB} or {@skill Survival|XPHB}) check, they notice several human-sized boot tracks leading away from the cave, along with clawed tracks. A successful {@dc 10} Intelligence ({@skill Nature|XPHB}) check can identify these tracks as those of a small dragon. One pair of human-sized tracks and one pair of slightly larger unbooted tracks lead into the cave, although steps were taken to hide them. These fainter tracks indicate that two creatures walked into the cave: the human-sized creature about four days ago, and the bugbear just hours ago.

{@b Studying the Cave Entrance}. By shining a light or peering into the cave entrance, the characters can get a better look at the damp area. A character taking the {@action Study|XPHB} action and succeeding on a {@dc 15} Intelligence ({@skill Investigation|XPHB}) check realizes the moisture isn't water: it's a greasy substance that might cause a person jumping into that area to slip when they land.

entries.name: Being the Characters' Senses
entries: As the Dungeon Master, you act as the characters' senses—they can't know something about the world or situation unless you tell them. Describe to the players everything their characters would experience, and check with the players occasionally to ensure everyone understands what their characters are sensing.

entries.name: Search vs. Study
entries: Two similar but different actions are {@action Search|XPHB} and {@action Study|XPHB}. Search is a Wisdom ability check that generally uses {@skill Perception|XPHB} to notice something or {@skill Survival|XPHB} to notice tracks. Study is an Intelligence ability check that uses skills like {@skill Investigation|XPHB} to interpret clues that have already been seen.

In this case, the characters can use a Search action to look around the area and notice things they might have missed on a first look. Those are handled with the D20 Test in "Searching the Area." The characters would use a Study action, as shown in "Studying the Cave Entrance," to realize that the moisture inside the cave is actually grease, which could cause a character to slip if they jump over the pit.

entries.name: Crossing the Pit
entries: A character with a Strength score of 10 or higher can jump over the pit if they get a running start of at least 10 feet. If they do this, however, they land on the greasy area (a trap laid down by the new master of these caves) and must succeed on a {@dc 10} Dexterity saving throw or slip on the grease and slide into the wall to the north of the pit, taking 3 ({@dice 1d6}) Bludgeoning damage and having the {@condition Prone|XPHB} condition until they stand up.

If a character attempts to cross the pit by climbing down into the pit or along the tunnel walls, they can do so without needing to make a {@variantrule D20 Test|XPHB} if they move at half speed, and they do not slip in the grease.

entries.name: Jumping
entries: When you make a {@variantrule Long Jump|XPHB}, you leap horizontally a number of feet up to your Strength score if you move at least 10 feet immediately before the jump. When you make a standing Long Jump, you can leap only half that distance. Either way, each foot you jump costs a foot of movement.

entries.name: A2: Hidden Bugbear
entries: When the first character crosses the pit, read or paraphrase the following:

There is a shadowy nook to the northeast of this area. A passageway runs to the east and then slowly bends to the southeast. The passageway also runs to the west and is then blocked by a beaded curtain.
The passageways lead to a cave blocked by a beaded curtain ({@area area A3|03e|x}) and a cave containing mostly junk ({@area area A4|043|x}).

entries.name: Hidden Bugbear
entries: Lugga, a {@creature Bugbear Warrior|XMM}, hides in the shadows of the nook. She moves into the hallway and attacks when the first character enters the greasy area. If the character(s) who crossed the pit first have a {@variantrule Passive Perception|XPHB} of 16 or higher, they are not {@status Surprised|XPHB}. Otherwise, they are Surprised. Roll initiative!

entries.name: Surprise
entries: If the character(s) crossing the pit don't notice the hiding bugbear, they're caught unawares by the start of combat and are Surprised. This means they have {@variantrule Disadvantage|XPHB} on their {@variantrule Initiative|XPHB} roll: they roll the {@dice d20} twice and must use the lower roll.

entries.name: Preparing for a Fight
entries: In this scene, the characters encounter a creature (Lugga the bugbear) who engages the characters in combat. Although the fight might end early, you still need to be prepared to run the fight! Here's how that plays out:

items.name: Describe the Scene
items.entry: Determine where everyone is standing at the start of the combat. Describe any other important details within the scene.

items.name: Roll Initiative
items.entry: The players each roll a {@dice d20} and add their Initiative modifier. The character with the highest result goes first, and the characters act in descending order. Lugga the bugbear acts on her Initiative roll within the Initiative order.

items.name: Take Turns
items.entry: On each of their turns, the characters and Lugga can move up to a distance equal to their {@variantrule Speed|XPHB}. Before, after, or during their movement, the characters or Lugga can use their action to take the {@action Attack|XPHB} action, take another action, or move up to their Speed again (the {@action Dash|XPHB} action.)

entries.name: Dealing with Lugga
entries: Although Lugga attacks initially, she is not unreasonable and can be reasoned with, as outlined below. She was kicked out of her clan for arguing with the leader, and she came here searching for other bugbears.

items: {@b Fighting with Lugga}. Lugga is {@variantrule Hostile [Attitude]|XPHB|Hostile} and attacks. She continues attacking until she is defeated, she is {@status Bloodied|XPHB}, or a character takes the {@action Influence|XPHB} action and succeeds on a {@dc 10} Charisma ({@skill Persuasion|XPHB} or {@skill Intimidation|XPHB}) check. At that point, carry on using "Talking with Lugga."

{@b Talking with Lugga}. Lugga surrenders if she is Bloodied. Alternatively, she stops attacking if a character succeeds on the Persuasion or Intimidation check to make her {@variantrule Indifferent [Attitude]|XPHB|Indifferent}.

entries: Whether she surrenders or stops attacking, Lugga tells the characters her story. She adds the following information if a character takes the Influence action again and succeeds a {@dc 15} Charisma ({@skill Persuasion|XPHB}) check to make her {@variantrule Friendly [Attitude]|XPHB|Friendly}:

items: Lugga heard strange moaning sounds to the west, behind the beaded curtain.
She also heard weapons clanging down the hallways to the southeast, but no talking or shouting like you might hear in typical combat.
Lugga just wants to leave this cave and find some friends.
If Lugga is Friendly, the characters can convince her to come with back to the keep after this adventure.

entries.name: Roleplaying Attitudes and Negotiations
entries: When you roleplay a character or creature, you determine how they think, act, and talk. Roleplaying is part of the whole game, but it comes to the fore during social interactions.

You should use an NPC's (nonplayer character's) personality, history, and goals—as well as the characters' actions—to determine how an NPC reacts to the characters. More importantly, the players use your portrayal of NPCs to learn their goals, then use that information to influence or interact with the NPCs.

If the characters offer an NPC something they want or play on their sympathies or fears, they can form friendships, ward off violence, or learn key information. Alternatively, If the characters insult or threaten or anger an NPC, that NPC might betray or attack the characters.

entries.name: A3: Kobold Zombies
entries: When the characters push past the beaded curtain into the room west of the passageway, read or paraphrase the following:

This cave contains heaps of waste. Two giant centipede corpses and four kobold bodies are strewn around the room. The kobold bodies, obviously dead, start to get to their feet, moaning something in their language.

These four kobold {@creature Zombie|XMM|Zombies} were magically animated and then discarded here by Groth the Wicked, a necromancer and alchemist. The zombies sense the characters when they enter the room, and they say "Brains" in the Draconic language. They fight until the characters defeat them, after which they're dead for good.

entries.name: Treasure
entries: Two {@item Potion of Healing|XDMG|Potions of Healing} were hidden by Groth the Wicked in the trash in this room.

entries.name: Adjusting Encounter Difficulty
entries: This adventure accommodates four or five players, each playing a character included with the adventure. If you have fewer than four or more than five players, this fight might be too difficult or too easy. For this encounter, make the following changes:
If there are fewer than four characters, remove one {@creature Zombie|XMM}.
If there are more than five characters, add one {@b Zombie}.

entries.name: A4: The Treasure Fights Back
entries: When the characters first see this room, read or paraphrase the following:
This area contains sleeping mats and piles of junk. Four shiny, spotless swords lie on the floor. Several lanterns light the area.
This room contains discarded junk from previous residents.

entries.name: Animated Swords
entries: The four {@creature Animated Flying Sword|XMM|Animated Flying Swords} remain motionless on the floor until a character approaches them or touches one. When that happens, the swords rise and attack.

entries.name: Groth Enters
entries: Groth the Wicked ({@creature Cultist|XMM}) is in the next room, examining the Zorn family dagger. When he hears combat in this area, he enters the initiative order and comes into the room on the second round to attack. He fights until he is dead.

entries.name: Playing Groth
entries: Groth is evil and selfish, caring only for growing his own power and doing his experiments. When he enters the room to fight the characters, you can show this by having him speak to the characters as he attacks. He could say something like "This cave is mine. Now that you know about it, I must get rid of you," or "Excellent! More fools who I can use for my experiments. I hope you like pain."

entries.name: Treasure
entries: One of the animated swords retains a small amount of magic after being defeated, giving the characters access to a {@item +1 Shortsword|XDMG}. Groth carries the Zorn family dagger.

entries.name: What if the Characters Go East First?
entries: If the characters go east before checking out the chamber ({@area area A3|03e|x}), the zombies do not emerge from there until the characters try to leave the dungeon.
Alternatively, if the characters are badly hurt, the zombies stay in area A3 and don't emerge. The next explorers find them instead!

entries.name: Adjusting Encounter Difficulty
entries: This adventure accommodates four or five players, each playing a character included with the adventure. If you have fewer than four or more than five players, the content might be too difficult or too easy. For this encounter, make the following changes:
•If there are fewer than four characters, remove one {@creature Animated Flying Sword|XMM}.
•If there are more than five characters, add one {@b Animated Flying Sword}.

entries.name: A5: Groth the Wicked's Experiment
entries: The characters likely enter this room after dealing with Groth in the previous cave. When they enter this room, read or paraphrase the following:

In this small room there is a makeshift laboratory. Among the beakers and burners is a single vial holding a purple liquid bubbling and boiling. Noxious green vapor leaks from the vial. The experiment looks like it's on the verge of exploding.
A gnome is chained to the wall in the corner of the room. He struggles to free himself, terrified.
This makeshift laboratory has been Groth's center of experimentation for the last week.

entries.name: Alchemical Experiment
entries: Groth is attempting to make a potion that makes the drinker resistant to Necrotic damage. His work was interrupted by the characters, and now the experiment is dangerous. The experiment explodes in three rounds unless the characters add the final ingredient to the mix: a sprig of wolfsbane. A character must take the {@action Study|XPHB} action and succeed on a {@dc 15} Intelligence ({@skill Arcana|XPHB} or {@skill Nature|XPHB}) check to know how to proceed. If the experiment explodes, each creature in room takes 7 ({@dice 2d6}) Acid damage. If the characters add wolfsbane, they complete the potion successfully (see "Treasure" below).

entries.name: Gnome Prisoner
entries: Groth kept this gnome named Gooster ({@creature Commoner|XMM}) as a prisoner, planning to use him as a test subject for the experiments. To free him, the character must take the {@action Utilize|XPHB} action and succeed on a {@dc 15} Dexterity ({@skill Sleight of Hand|XPHB}) check using {@item Thieves' Tools|XPHB} to unlock the restraints. A character can also use an action and succeed on a {@dc 15} Strength ({@skill Athletics|XPHB}) check to break the chains. The chains have a 10 AC, 15 Hit Points, and a {@variantrule damage threshold|XPHB} of 5.

entries.name: Treasure
entries: Groth's workshop contains 100 GP. If the characters successfully finish the alchemical project Groth started, they have a {@item Potion of Necrotic Resistance|XDMG}. Gooster additionally gives the group 20 GP for rescuing him.

entries.name: Taking On a Noncombat Challenge
entries: At some points in a D&D game, you might have a challenge that isn't a combat but is still tense and exciting. In this case, the characters need to quickly finish the potion and rescue the gnome before experiment explodes.

As the players brainstorm their ideas, engage them in a discussion. If the idea is preposterous, like smashing the alchemical equipment as a means of finishing the experiment, it's okay to say the idea won't work. Ideas are provided in the adventure text, but alternative methods can be allowed if they make sense.

items.name: Using the Help Action
items.entry: Characters who are not actively making the ability checks can use the {@action Help|XPHB} action on their turns to assist those who are trying to finish the experiment or free the gnome. By assisting in this way, the helping character chooses the character they're going to assist. That character has {@variantrule Advantage|XPHB} on their ability check or attack roll.

entries.name: Damaging Objects
entries: A creature or an object that has a {@variantrule damage threshold|XPHB} has Immunity to all damage unless it takes an amount of damage from a single attack or effect equal to or greater than its damage threshold, in which case it takes that entire instance of damage. Any damage that fails to meet or exceed the damage threshold is superficial and doesn't reduce {@variantrule Hit Points|XPHB}. In this case, the chain takes no damage unless at least 5 damage of any type is dealt to it, at which point it takes the full damage and those Hit Points are deducted from the chain's Hit Point total.

entries.name: Conclusion
entries: If the characters succeed in their mission, they can return to the Keep on the Borderlands with the Zorn family dagger. Quintanna Zorn eventually meets them at the Traveler's Inn to pay their reward for the dagger.

entries.name: Now What?!
entries: If the characters complete this adventure successfully, you have many options to continue their adventures. Here are just a few. All of these are available digitally or physically on {@adventure D&D Beyond|HotDQ} and at your local game store.
Check out the {@adventure D&D Starter Set: Heroes of the Borderlands|HotB}, which is thematically linked to this adventure and has everything you need to start a D&D game with new players!
Pick up the {@book Player's Handbook|XPHB} to get a solid foundation for all sorts of adventures!
For an anthology of adventures featuring dragons, {@adventure Dragon Delves|DrDe-DaS} offers play for levels 1-12!
If you want to dive into a D&D setting, {@book Forgotten Realms: Adventures in Faerûn|FRAiF} has a whole world to explore!
If spooky games are fun for you and your players, {@link Ravenloft: The Horrors Within|https://marketplace.dndbeyond.com/category/6015000} offers frightfully good horror-themed inspiration!

items.entry: John Grello, Dario Jelusic, Brian Valeza
